turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in Case 14ยญ116, Bullard v. Blue Hills Bank. Mr. Feldman.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: A denial of a confirmation of a bankruptcy plan is appealable as of right under 28 U.S.C. Section 158, the bankruptcyยญspecific provision that deals with appeals in bankruptcy cases as opposed to other civil cases. That is because the denial of confirmation finally determines the debtor's right to the disposition of property and future income that's provided for in the plan that was denied.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: How often, when you have a plan denied, is there typically ยญยญ okay, we'll try again, the judge says, I don't like this part of it, you address it and fix it. In other words, you ยญยญ you try again, or maybe a third time, until you get it. Okay, now it's perfect. Let's go ahead.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I would say very frequently that occurs.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I'm sorry, very ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Very frequently.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, if it occurs very frequently, why does it make sense to allow an appeal from the first denial? Why not wait until ยญยญ why isn't it proceeding, the consideration of whether to confirm the plan, and you wait until it's all done, and the judge says, I'm not going to do this, I'm done, rather than ยญยญ if ยญยญ if you go through three versions of the plan, under your theory, you would have the right to appeal each one.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Well, I ยญยญ you would have the right to appeal each one. I don't think anybody would ever do it. They ยญยญ there are many, many incentives for debtors, especially in Chapter 13, to not go ahead with appeals. But they want to get a plan confirmed and move on with their lives, and they don't have the ยญยญ the ยญยญ the money or the time or anything else to go through appeals of every issue. The importance of this is not for your ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The problem is that that may be because they never thought they had the right to appeal ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I ยญยญ I don't ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: ยญยญ because it's very easy to get an interlocutory ruling if there's ยญยญ if the rejection of the plan is on a legal question. I still don't understand why you didn't go that route.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I ยญยญ actually, we did go that route.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yeah. And the circuit said ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: And the ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: ยญยญ you already filed your appeal, so we don't have to.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: The bankruptcy appellate panel said, for a reason that I don't understand, that they just didn't want to certify it to the court of appeals. They did certify it to themselves ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is it ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: ยญยญ but ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: ยญยญ is it only the ยญยญ the debtor who can appeal a rejection of a plan?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I think our view would be, yes, it's only the debtor ยญยญ well, only the proponent of the plan. And in Chapter 11, after a period of exclusivity, it could be a creditor who proposed the plan.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And what we say for 13 would apply to 11, as well, I assume?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I ยญยญ I think as a ยญยญ as a general matter, that's probably correct. This is a 13 case and not an 11 case, but I think as a generally ยญยญ the question in both cases would be: What is the nature of the proceeding? And it's similar, but not identical in both cases. So it would turn on how ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, in 11, you ยญยญ you would have some wellยญheeled litigants who would ยญยญ would ยญยญ would appeal ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I ยญยญ I think ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: ยญยญ two or three times.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Actually, I ยญยญ I think it's unlikely in 11 also. The ยญยญ the debtor in 11 is somebody who usually wants to get ยญยญ they have a huge incentive to get the company ยญยญ you're trying to reorganize the company, and they're trying to get this company through the 11 process. It's very hard to operate in a Chapter 11. More ยญยญ they're trying to get it through as quickly as possible, and taking an appeal is just an impediment to that ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, what about ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: ยญยญ but ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: ยญยญ what about the other side? You say the other side could appeal.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: A creditor ยญยญ creditors also actually want their money. They usually want the plan ยญยญ want ยญยญ they want a plan to go into effect. The kinds of appeals in both 11 and especially in 13 that are going to happen is not where the court says, well, you know, if you give another $5 a week out of your ยญยญ your projected ยญยญ you have to devote your whole projected disposable income to plan in 13. If you give another $5 a week, I ยญยญ that I would approve it. Debtors are going to ยญยญ all of those are going to be modified, and they're going go through like that.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So it's only in the important cases where this is a possibility?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: ยญยญ yes, but it's cases that are important to the debtor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, if it's an important case, then the avenues that are provided ยญยญ the ยญยญ the multiple avenues that are provided for an interlocutory appeal would seem to be satisfied.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I ยญยญ I don't think that's right. Interlocutory appeal serves a fundamentally different purpose than appeal as of right. Interlocutory appeal is generally for the benefit of the system in systematically important cases. But we rely on the parties for issues that are important to them and they also help with the development of the law to have appeal as of right. The debtor may feel that their house ยญยญ in fact, the debtor's house frequently is at stake. I would say most or a majority or a lot of Chapter 13 cases.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I ยญยญ I don't understand.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: And if they're going to lose the house ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: What would be the purpose of all those alternative interlocutory appeal if we accept your argument that in every contested matter a decision is final?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: We ยญยญ we haven't argued that every contested matter would result in a right to appeal. It would depend on what the contested matter is. But the point of those is just the kinds of ยญยญ if you look up the kinds of things that have been certified for interlocutory appeals. The Court ยญยญ there is discovery disputes in bankruptcy cases that have going to interlocutory appeals that nobody thinks would be appealable as of right. There is denials of motions to dismiss the case that nobody thinks is appealable as of right, but the courts have taken on interlocutory appeals. The attorney disqualification ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: If we interpreted the ยญยญ the statute to say that there is jurisdiction here appeals from final judgments and final orders, do you ยญยญ is your case still just as strong? Is this, in your view, a final order?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Yes it's a final order, because it finally determined the debtor's rights to the property ยญยญ the disposition of property and future income that's provided for on the plan that's now settled and it's not going to be litigated again.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, the first thing the judge said is that I want you to file an amended plan ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: That's what I don't understand.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: ยญยญ in a month.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So at least the judge didn't think it was done and over.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: No, the judge thought for this plan, it was over. I think everybody agrees that the judge thought that this debtor can't get what the debtor wanted in this plan. He can't get this treatment ยญยญ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, I guess it, again, maybe ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: ยญยญ but ยญยญ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: ยญยญ the way you look at it is whether the proceeding is the confirmation or denial of this plan, or if the proceeding is whether to confirm or deny a plan. Right?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Yes, yes. That ยญยญ that's right. And the ยญยญ the point is that ยญยญ that the denial of this plan kept the debtor from getting this particular relief that he wanted and that is finished and not going to be litigated ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: If ยญยญ if the debtor had wanted to file a ยญยญ a new plan to try and satisfy the judge's concerns, would he have had to file a new action with a new number?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: No. It wouldn't have had to be a new ยญยญ but ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, then why is it a final order? That's what I don't understand.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Well, because in bankruptcy, you ยญยญ you know, there are many, many appeals that are allowed by the ยญยญ in bankruptcy including appeals of priority among creditors that don't ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And are these all final orders?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Yes. They don't ยญยญ they don't complete the whole bankruptcy case. The point in bankruptcy, and Congress enshrined this in Section 158, is you look at it proceeding by proceeding. And the question is when the proceeding ended ยญยญ ends. And the only way to give the debtor an effective right to appeal at all, really, is to allow the debtor ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Why? Why ยญยญ why doesn't the debtor just say ยญยญ Judge says, debtor, I don't like your plan. I'm not confirming it. But if you amend it in this way, that way, or the other way, then I'll probably confirm it.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay? And then ยญยญ and then the debtor says, I don't want to amend it that way. Okay? I'm not doing it. No amendment filed. Then what happens?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Then I think that the court probably dismiss the case.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Fine, and then the debtor has an appeal.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So what's the problem?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: So here's the problem: If you require the debtor ยญยญ so the debtor here has been denied the right to the hybrid treatment of his mortgage that he wanted in a plan. If you require the debtor to go to a dismissal on the entire bankruptcy case in order to appeal that, the debtor and other creditors lose the right to the automatic stay.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Oh, is that a good idea? What we do is we do there the same as any other case. We say I appreciate your staying this so I don't lose the advantage while I have an opportunity to appeal. And if the bankruptcy judge doesn't do it, you ask the appellate panel to do it. That comes every day in the week, it comes up in criminal cases, civil cases all the time.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Right, but at that point ยญยญ at that point the debtor's burden in showing that they're entitled to a stay, courts have viewed that as a similar ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Does he have a good reason or not?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I'm ยญยญ he does have a good reason, but ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay. Then he'll get it.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: But ยญยญ well ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Courts make mistakes sometimes, by the way.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But it seems to me that to guard against a potential mistake, to potentially have appeals ยญยญ potentially anyway, none of us knows ยญยญ in dozens of cases, and then you say contested matters, there are all kinds of contested matters so we'll have to invent a new set of distinctions about which contested matters do count as final and which ones don't. That seems to me to be throwing a very giant baby out with the bath water.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Your Honor, we're not arguing that all contested matters should be appealable. We're arguing that denials of plan confirmation are ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And they're the only ones. And then ยญยญ then fee ยญยญ fee matters or not, though they're contested matters. I have a list of about 47 contested matters here, that Collier has, and it seemed to me I couldn't figure out. Maybe that's my failing, but I couldn't figure out what, under your rule, which contested matters were and which were not.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: It's give ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Right. There's no ยญยญ

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: It's a given, isn't it, the ยญยญ the confirmation of a plan, the confirmation of a plan can be appealed?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Yes, confirmation of a plan can be appealed.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And I thought your argument was that this is just the flip side. You can confirm or you can deny. Creditors can appeal if ยญยญ if it's confirmed.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So you're defining the contest as yes or no to this plan.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Right. And the very same issue would be at issue if, had the court confirmed it, as it would be at issue in this appeal of the denial ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I didn't see that. Because I thought when a court confirms a plan then there is nothing left to do but execute the plan. So obviously what you have to do is give an appeal, or the plan will be executed, the wages will be garnished, you'll be paying forever. But when you deny the plan, nothing more will happen and, therefore, there is appeal.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: No ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Now that's what I thought the way it worked.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Actually ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I didn't see why you give an additional set of appeals when only an amendment is at stake.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: But ยญยญ I guess, let me address first the issue of amendment. The ยญยญ the ยญยญ to be talking about this as leave ยญยญ the question is what is a proceeding, not what is a contested matter. And everybody agrees that you can have proceedings that are smaller than the bankruptcy case and the question is whether this is one of them or not. Now the fact that the court granted leave to amend is really equivalent in the norm or similar in a normal civil case, to a court denying without prejudice. It means, yes, debtor, you can come back with another plan. You are permitted to do that. That starts a new proceeding. The ยญยญ At that point the debtor files the plan, the creditor objects, you have whatever litigation you need over that plan. The judge holds a hearing just like he held the hearing before, and ultimately the judge decides whether to grant or deny confirmation. And that is ยญยญ that looks like a proceeding. It has a beginning and a middle and an end and it follows a series of steps and comes to a conclusion on that. The ยญยญ the fact that in a civil, in a normal civil case, of course when a judge denies with leave to ยญยญ a court dismisses a complaint with leave to amend frequently on the ground just that the complaint is defective or something. But when a court does that, you're operating under 1291 where you're not looking for what a proceeding is. Maybe in a normal civil case that would be seen as a proceeding. But that's not the question that you ask. But that is the question that you ask here. As far as stays, courts are reluctant to ยญยญ it's one thing ยญยญ a debtor gets an automatic stay when they file their bankruptcy petition. Courts have ยญยญ look ยญยญ view an attempt to get a stay of a dismissal, they generally judge those under the standards for preliminary injunction which is not easy to meet. You're going to the judge ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Does that mean that they would have to say that there's a likelihood that their own ruling is wrong?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Yes. They ยญยญ Yes, it does. And they're very reluctant to do that. The automatic stay is a very powerful and important thing. But the important ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Because if Justice Breyer were right, that that's a possibility, you would think that that would be a very good way to solve this problem. But you're just saying that they're ยญยญ the automatic stays are going to disappear on most of these debtors?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Yes. And also the debtor, I would say also if the debtor, there's actually four different disabilities that a debtor would have if the case is dismissed. But one of the most important parts of it is, it's not just the debtor who gets harmed by that, it's other creditors. If the debtor doesn't get the automatic stay other creditors are going to also have problems because they'll start grabbing property, and the whole point of the bankruptcy could be lost while the appeal is going ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Am I ยญยญ am I correct in my recollection that your brief says that some jurisdictions have adopted the principle that you're urging and the sky has not fallen?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: That's right. The Fifth Circuit, the Third, Fourth and Fifth Circuits have all adopted rules that are similar to what we're proposing here.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What happened on the other side?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: You actually ยญยญ with ยญยญ we haven't found any case where ยญยญ in one of the other six circuits where a debtor has โ a ยญยญ sought a ยญยญ a stay ยญยญ let me get this right ยญยญ where ยญยญ where a debtor has ยญยญ has got ยญยญ had a dismissal and sought to extend the automatic stay so he can take an appeal of a dismissal.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Did they all lose their houses or something in the interim?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: That ยญยญ that is what happens

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Do we know that?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Actually, I don't know what ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I mean, that's a pretty ยญยญ I mean, you know, I realize judges are difficult, but it seems to me if somebody was going to lose his house because you wouldn't grant a stay where he has some kind of an issue on appeal, that's a pretty appealing case for issuing that stay.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I think courts ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Or they're rather hardยญhearted if they don't ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Courts have kind of a uniform ยญยญ I think have uniformly applied the standard that you have to show a likelihood of success. Not a possibility of success, but a likelihood of success, which is very hard to show once you've just been denied ยญยญ once the court has just dismissed the case ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: There is no loseยญyourยญhouse exception to that?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Not that I know of. But there is ยญยญ there is three other ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I agree if there is some merit to your appeal ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: There is ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But are we then turning this on whether there's some ยญยญ you want to give a lot of appeals with no merit?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I don't think that you will, and the Fifth Circuits have had this rule since 2000. We found three cases I think in the court of appeals, three other cases in the district court. Since then, the cases don't ยญยญ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Reported cases, or any cases?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Reported cases, or any cases?

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: All the cases we were able to find on Westlaw which involved reported and unreported. It didn't involve all cases. No. โUm โ The ยญยญ

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: How much ยญยญ how much time, Mr. Feldman, would be involved? So nothing is going to go on while this issue goes up on appeal.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: That ยญยญ it's actually ยญยญ that's only kind of true. In a Chapter 13 case, the debtor has to start making payments as soon as the plan is proposed, which is in about 30 days or so of the time the petition is filed. And the debtor makes payments usually for three to five years. So I would say in virtually every case and probably every case while the appeal is going on, the debtor is still making the payments under the plan. The ยญยญ it is true the money isn't disbursed to the creditor until after there is an ultimate confirmation, but meanwhile, the ยญยญ as I think you just heard in the other case, the trustee is supposed to invest the money in an interestยญbearing account. Those proceeds will eventually go to the creditors. So things are ยญยญ in a Chapter 13 case ยญยญ are going on in the course of the appeal.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Something about your claim, yes, you can amend. But as I understood, your claim is ยญยญ the answer you've been given is never. You cannot have a hybrid.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Yes. That is what ยญยญ

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: This plan ยญยญ

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: ยญยญ to never amend ยญยญ to make a little change here or there. It just can't happen at all.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: And those cases which involve issues that are really of importance, are of importance to the debtor, and perhaps importance to the whole bankruptcy system, are the ones that are going to be appealed and that won't ever be appealed if you don't allow an appeal at this stage. And I think as shown by the presence of the United States and Bank of America, both of which, I think, are probably among the largest creditors in the country and probably participate as creditors in more bankruptcy cases than anyone else, on our side of the case, this ยญยญ allowing an appeal at this stage is ยญยญ is important for the sake of sound functionality of the bankruptcy system for allowing bankruptcy ยญยญ appellate courts to develop and harmonize the law in bankruptcy, which is a need that many observers have noted. And it won't do any undue harm to creditors because the ยญยญ especially in a Chapter 13 case, the creditor is ยญยญ the debtor is continuing to pay, and in any case, the cases that are going to be appealed are not the ones where you can negotiate a small difference. Parties have incentives, especially debtors ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You have ยญยญ

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: ยญยญ to get these cases ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: My problem still remains. You have multiple ways of getting an interlocutory appeal. You can go to the bankruptcy court. You can go to the back court. You can go to the district court. And you can even ask the court of appeals to ยญยญ to grant one.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: You do, but the ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So you have four levels of protection in the event that a ยญยญ one court, like the one you applied to, says no to an interlocutory appeal.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: But I would just say that those are designed for the exceptional case as ยญยญ where they're used, not for classes of cases like denials ยญยญ denials of confirmation where the debtor ยญยญ if something is important to that policy ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But if you have a novel issue of law like this one, you had all of those avenues.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Right. But and ยญยญ and we didn't actually get the certification, and it's also a burden on the courts of appeals and on the various courts to deal with all of the certifications. It adds an additional level of overhead.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, that's probably because there aren't that many denials that are based on novel issues of law.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: It ยญยญ it can be based on the ยญยญ the ยญยญ the debtors losing their house. If I could reserve the balance of my time.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Tripp, welcome. SUPPORTING THE PETITIONER

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: To just pick up on a couple of the questions. In the ยญยญ in the circumstance after there has already been a dismissal of the case, it's ยญยญ and this is in our ยญยญ in our brief at page 28. The ยญยญ the courts treat that as an affirmative injunction, right? Because that's an injunction against collection efforts by third parties that would need to be put in place. That's an extraordinary remedy, and ยญยญ and were not available at any circumstance in which that ยญยญ that has been granted. And then the second piece, just to pick up quickly on the safety valves. We just want to ยญยญ two points about that. One, is we're not asking for ยญยญ this is not a case like Mohawk, where we're asking for an exception to the ordinary rule of finality because it's ยญยญ it's really important to take an ordinary ยญยญ an appeal soon. What we're saying is we want to apply the ordinary rule as written because the order here ended a discrete dispute, and so there is ยญยญ there is an appeal by right under ยญยญ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: But that just begs the question. Is ยญยญ if the discrete dispute is the confirmation of this particular plan, yes. If the discrete dispute is consideration of the plan that is going to be part of the bankruptcy, then what you just said is wrong.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Right. And I think that's absolutely the nub of the case is trying to determine whether this is a discrete dispute, and ยญยญ and so it's not about whether it's ยญยญ it's substantive rights or procedural. It's really ยญยญ it's whether it's discrete. And I think the path we suggest in our brief which is drawn from the Collier treatise is really by far the clearest way of getting at this. So what we say is ยญยญ is first, you just recognize that a bankruptcy case is ยญยญ consists of adversary proceedings in contested matters, and then you just apply ordinary principles of finality to those contested matters. And that will tell you three things. It will tell you that ยญยญ that there's ยญยญ so it will tell you that any order that did not end the adversary proceeding or contested matter, you know that's not final, and that could only be taken up as an interlocutory appeal. You know that it's ended a dispute because there's been a dispute, and then you know that it's at least formally distinct, right? Because you have this formally distinct adversary proceeding or contested matter.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But I think that the intuition ยญยญ

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: But if I could just follow up ยญยญ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: If you're going to talk about the formality, the bankruptcy judge who doesn't want to be appealed, and I assume most of them don't, simply has to say, you know, I think I will probably deny this unless you come back with something and tell me that ยญยญ why this creditor shouldn't get, you know, 200 dollars as opposed to 10 dollars. And then, although in substance, it's exactly the same as here, where he says this one's denied, but come back with another one ยญยญ

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: And yet I assume you'd have to say there are different results in those two cases.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Right. And ยญยญ and under Respondent's own rule, if that order comes out the other way, creditors have an absolute right to take that appeal over a $100 difference, over a $1 difference, they can two levels of appeal straight to the court of appeals. And the creditors make that own judgment, and I think if there was a reason ยญยญ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but creditors want to get on with it. They want to get paid and out of it. I mean, the problem here is that we're dealing with bankruptcy where speed is of the essence. I mean, you've got dissipating assets and ยญยญ and all that.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: And yet, your creditors aren't terribly interested in delaying things. They want to move forward.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: No. And I think neither are debtors. Everybody has a very strong incentive to move on, and I think that's one of the reasons, you know ยญยญ if there was a serious concern that there was going to be a flood of these ยญยญ of these debtor appeals about $10, I think, frankly, you wouldn't see us supporting Petitioner, or you wouldn't see Bank of America supporting Petitioner. You'd expect to see creditor groups supporting Respondent.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, why isn't there, I mean, just the converse of what's been argued? The debtor is ยญยญ there's an automatic stay. He's living in his house every day. He's submitting plans. Let's say, the lawyer decides one every three days. They're not dismissed, but they say, do it ยญยญ do it over again. And I think this is great. Now, I'll appeal each one. And then I live in my house for ten years, because it takes a long time to resolve that. I'm not saying that that's good or bad, but I just wonder why ยญยญ apparently, that hasn't happened in any of the these circuits, and you explain to me why.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Right. I think there's a number of reasons. One is that as soon as you propose a plan under 1326(a), within 30 days of that, you need to start making your payments. And if you're not making adequate protection payments to your secured creditors, then it doesn't matter what you're saying with your plan. Your entire case can and should be dismissed. And so I think we also ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: In other words, they would catch abuses.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: They would ยญยญ they would catch abuses. And ยญยญ and the other way to catch abuses is ยญยญ is not only with respect to secured creditors, but ยญยญ but if you propose an absurd plan, then ยญยญ then that would be valid grounds for stay relief on behalf of ยญยญ of a creditor who was coming in to complain.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: My example uses absurd plans to make a point, but the point is just as good where the plans are not absurd, but plausible, but there's this, that, and the other thing. They're borderline cases. They do and can take time. You could and might proliferate appeals, so why hasn't that happened? I'm ยญยญ that's the question. Why hasn't that happened?

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Right. I think the number one reason is that nobody ยญยญ nobody ยญยญ somebody who is bankrupt and is going into Chapter 13 to save their house or their car or their bank account from imminent collection efforts isn't going to spend thousands of dollars they don't have taking an appeal to fight about what to do with $100. The cases that we're most concerned about here ยญยญ and this is where Respondent's rule is really most perverse ยญยญ the most important cases where it's really dispositive as to whether you're going to be able to save your house, your car, or your bank account, they're saying first you need to dismiss the case and potentially lose your house, your car, or your bank account before you can even take the appeal.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Or ยญยญ or you ask for an interlocutory appeal.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: And ยญยญ and ยญยญ and I ยญยญ I think, frankly, as I was saying before about the interlocutory appeals, we don't ยญยญ we don't think that that's the right way to look at it because we're not asking for an expectation. The other piece ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But do you think ยญยญ

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: ยญยญ you know, Respondent ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: ยญยญ just ยญยญ just ยญยญ

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: ยญยญ would the interlocutory appeal route work? I mean, especially if this Court were to say in an opinion, you know, interlocutory appeals are really good for this exact purpose. Is there anything in the structure of the system, is there anything in the incentives of judges that would prevent them from doing interlocutory appeals?

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: I ยญยญ I think a couple different answers to that. Some of these cases are going to be mixed questions of law and fact which are going to be difficult to take up in an interlocutory appeal. Creditors already have the right to take up the mixed ยญยญ mixed ยญยญ mixed question cases as of right, however they want to take them. And then I think the other answer is, it's ยญยญ it's, frankly, completely backward to think of 158(d) as a sort of ยญยญ as ยญยญ as a reason to say you should allow more ยญยญ more ยญยญ as a reason to ยญยญ to read finality relatively narrowly here, because Congress enacted 158(d) to expand the ยญยญ the path to appeal beyond that which 158(a) already had, because Congress in 2005 was concerned not that there were too many bankruptcy appeals, but there were too few ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I ยญยญ I didn't understand ยญยญ

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: ยญยญ and the problem ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: ยญยญ the first part of your answer to ยญยญ to Justice Kagan. You ยญยญ you said, well, there's some cases where the interlocutory appeals won't work because it's ยญยญ it's partly factual and so forth. But those are the very cases you say there's an automatic right. Your position is there's a right to appeal. You don't even need the interlocutory orders.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Well ยญยญ well, the ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So it seems to me you're saying that now there are going to appeals in ยญยญ in ยญยญ under your view, that really shouldn't be taken at all. It seems to me that works against you.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: No, no, no. That's ยญยญ that's not what we're saying. What we're saying is that creditors have to right to make the judgment of whether it's important enough to them to take the appeal ยญยญ I'm sorry ยญยญ debtors should have that right. Creditors already that right. When it's a mixed question of law and fact, they can take two levels of appeals. They can take it all the way to the court of appeals. But if ยญยญ if on the ยญยญ on the way up, if a ยญยญ if a court of appeals rules in favor of the creditor, and now the debtor wants to take another appeal, this is exact ยญยญ the ยญยญ that ยญยญ and the debtor wants to take another appeal, then they're stuck, because on Respondent's rule, it becomes an interlocutory ruling ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The ยญยญ the ยญยญ

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: ยญยญ even though it's substantially symmetrical. It's either the plan will go into operation, or it will never go into operation.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The intuition that you're running up against here, Mr. Tripp ยญยญ

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: ยญยญ and the reason why people are searching to see if there are alternatives to your rule, is this idea that you're shortยญcircuit ยญยญ shortยญcircuiting what ought to be a kind of negotiated outcome. And, you know, what I ยญยญ what I hear you saying is just, no, you're not, because nobody will ever want to shortยญcircuit a negotiated outcome. Is that ยญยญ is that your answer?

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: I think in the situations where a negotiated outcome is ยญยญ is fairly available, the incentives to reach that are overwhelming. They're very, very, very strong. And I think that's one of the reasons we just haven't seen a flood of appeals and one of the reasons that ยญยญ that both the United States and Bank of America is here for Petitioner and ยญยญ and nobody's come out to support Respondent. And, I mean, I think, the ยญยญ the other ยญยญ just ยญยญ just to think about the ยญยญ the intuition here, a ยญยญ a plan order is ยญยญ is basically in the nature of an injunction. It's saying that we want to put this thing into effect on the ground. And for more than a century, even in ordinary civil litigation, the rule is that an order granting an injunction or an order denying an injunction is immediately appealable by right, and that you don't need to ask permission in order to take that appeal.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's a symmetrical argument, but we're asking a practical argument. I'm not ยญยญ

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: ยญยญ sure I accept the symmetrical argument, but I have to think about it. The practical argument is imagine you're a bankruptcy judge, and you get a plan, and it's not perfect, and you think, you know, they ought to change this, they ought to change that, they ought to change the other thing, it would be okay. Now, if you say, go change it, go change it, I deny this one, but go change it, well, you'd have to take into account, gee, maybe I'll get an appeal. God, this is going to slow everything down. It's just simpler to say, figure out some way around this. I'm afraid, in other words, that the bankruptcy judges will ยญยญ would get confused on your approach. Is there a concern there that the negotiation will be made more difficult?

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: May I still answer?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: TRIPP
turns.text: I'm ยญยญ I'm not sure I understand. Get confused about when the order becomes final?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No, forget it. Forget it.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. HallwardยญDriemeier. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I agree with Your Honor's question, Mr. Chief Justice, that confirmation is sort of the central focus of the Chapter 13 proceeding, and, in fact, speed is of the essence; and, Justice Kagan, your question suggesting that it is supposed to be, in ยญยญ in many respects, a negotiated process. And suggesting and ยญยญ and creating an opportunity for debtors to take an immediate appeal for two layers, as of right, perhaps multiple times through the confirmation process would dramatically change that dynamic.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The ยญยญ who ยญยญ who has the incentive to do that? The incentive is someone like you, who has a pure legal question. But they're saying there isn't an incentive to do that in a Chapter 13. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I think there are incentives. There are both offensive incentives and defensive incentives: Defensive, first, the ยญยญ the person who is trying to save their home and thinks that they'll be able to stay there longer if they're able to take multiple appeals, each of which could take two years; offensively, by taking an appeal or at least threatening to take an appeal that would delay the process for years significantly increases the leverage that the debtor has over the creditors in terms of the negotiating. It dramatically shifts ยญยญ

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Do we have any ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ the ยญยญ

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Do we have any history, in the circuits that have the rule, that you can appeal of right from a denial of a plan proposal? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: There ยญยญ there is not much history to look at here. The ยญยญ the Fifth Circuit was the first to suggest that there might be a right to appeal, but they actually held that that was only true where there was no leave to amend. So in our case, it would not have allowed an immediate right of appeal. The ยญยญ the Third and Fourth Circuits have adopted the rule relatively recently, since 2005, after Congress amended the statute last. When Congress amended it, all of the courts of appeals that had clearly ruled had ruled in our way. So ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, 2005 ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ we don't have ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: ยญยญ that's, you know, 10 years. And these cases are coming up all of the time, and it seems as though you have a good natural experiment that goes on here. And ยญยญ and it hasn't really led to the kinds of bad consequences that we're all ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: ยญยญ surmising about. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ I ยญยญ I think there are ยญยญ there are certainly potential reasons for that. One is none of those circuits have a bankruptcy appellate panel, and there is a general impression, rightly or wrongly, that bankruptcy judges are more debtorยญfriendly than Article III judges. And so the opportunity to take an immediate appeal as of right to a panel of three other bankruptcy judges may well be more ยญยญ more attractive to a debtor than going to an Article III judge who might well be perceived as being less hospitable than the bankruptcy judge ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: When ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ than you might ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: When you argue as ยญยญ as you've just argued that this would give the debtors too much unfair leverage, how does that account for the fact that some of the very major creditors in the country on the Petitioner's side? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, Your Honor, I think ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: I mean, they must not think there's too much leverage. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, Your Honor, the ยญยญ the rule that Congress has adopted about the ยญยญ the safety valve, if you will, for getting issues reviewed in the courts of appeals gives the ultimate control of the docket to the court, not the parties. Even if the parties agree that the issue should go to the court of appeals, the court of appeals has to agree to accept it.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Do we know why ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: It's not surprising to me at all ยญยญ

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Do we know why, in this case, the ยญยญ the bankruptcy ยญยญ whatever you call it ยญยญ the BAP, they would not certify an appeal? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I think that, first, they gave a procedural basis, that the ยญยญ the debtor had already filed the notice of appeal before they sought leave to appeal, and ยญยญ and they cited that. But they also, I think, recognized that at that point, four bankruptcy judges had already ruled against the ยญยญ the debtor. The ยญยญ the First Circuit looked like at this ยญยญ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Counsel, just to ยญยญ you were winding up to answer Justice Kennedy's question, and ยญยญ and you haven't ยญยญ you didn't throw the pitch. So what is your answer to his question?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: About ยญยญ about why the Bank of America and the United States are on the other side. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I ยญยญ I think that a good reason is that they would rather that the control over ability to take an appeal be in the parties' hands, rather than the court's hands, which is where Congress put it in terms of the interlocutory appeal avenue.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: But that ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, it's already in their hands, isn't it, when ยญยญ when ยญยญ when it's a confirmation of a plan, right? It's in the ยญยญ it's in their hands, in the parties' hands. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: That ยญยญ that's right because ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: They ยญยญ they ยญยญ they want it to be in the debtor's hands? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: As ยญยญ as the Court held in Espinosa, when a ยญยญ a plan is confirmed, it has immediate legal effect, res judicata effect even. So clearly that is appealable. There is no res judicata effect, or at least it's never been thought that there was from the denial of a plan ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So why do they want the debtor to have control of bringing an appeal? That's what I'm asking. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, if we actually look ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You really don't know. It doesn't make any sense, does it? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: The Bank of America brief, I have to say, is somewhat surprising to me, because the rationale that they give is that in their case, which is also pending here on the petition for cert., they won in the district court, but that's not good enough for them. They want to force the court of appeals to decide this issue so that they have controlling circuit preference.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What ยญยญ what is the ยญยญ why is it that a debtor can't ยญยญ a creditor can't appeal? I mean, suppose you had a plan, the proposed plan said, I want to give $200 a month to Smith and a $100 to Jones, and the bankruptcy judge said, I'm sorry, I cannot approve that. I want it the other way around. Well, Smith is hurt by what he said, so why can't Smith appeal? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Your Honor, I agree, the ยญยญ the Petitioner answered the question earlier suggesting that only the debtor could appeal. If the determination of this Court is that the order denying confirmation with leave to appeal is a final order, it would follow, I would think, that there is res judicata effect of that order just as Espinosa held, and that not only could a creditor appeal, the creditor must appeal in order to preserve their rights. And when one thinks about this in the ยญยญ how it would apply in the Chapter 11 context, when you don't simply have one plan, at least after the period of exclusivity has expired, but rather multiple plans, the debtor has suggested and the creditors have proposed theirs, it raises very serious questions of what the bankruptcy court is to do. While one plan that's been denied confirmation is up on appeal, we have all these other plans that are pending in the bankruptcy court. Congress envisioned that that was going to be the period of negotiation. That there would be ultimately a consensus plan. In bankruptcy, perhaps above any other circumstance, the right plan is very often not any party's first plan; it's everybody's second plan. And I want to make clear, again, that there are actually three avenues for a debtor whose proposed plan has been denied. The first, as the Court has discussed already, is to seek leave for interlocutory appeal. The Ransom case, which has already been discussed earlier today, is an example of that. The question there was whether automobile ownership costs were an allowable expense when the ยญยญ the debtor was not making payments on a car. That issue was ยญยญ the plan was denied confirmation on that basis, the bankruptcy court certified it for interlocutory appeal, it came up through the court of appeals ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But Mr. HallwardยญDriemeier ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ all the way to this Court.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: ยญยญ I mean, you have to admit that it's very unpredictable. Some judges will do it, some judges won't. It won't be completely clear when they do it or why they do it, it will just be kind of luck of the draw. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: And ยญยญ And ยญยญ indeed the amicus briefs complain that Congress's solution to this problem which was to expand the avenues for interlocutory appeal are inadequate because courts aren't giving leave freely enough. That, Your Honors, is not a reason to adopt a rule of finality that puts the power in the parties' ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, yes it is. I mean, we're trying to figure out what's the best alternative of these systems in a world in which we're not particularly limited by text. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So it's quite important how the other alternatives work. And if the interlocutory appeal alternative really isn't working because courts aren't using it for these kinds of purposes, then that's an important factor to know about, isn't it? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I think that ยญยญ I think that the proper reaction to that would be, as Your Honor's question earlier suggested, to instruct the courts that they should apply it more liberally. That that ยญยญ that's the way to gauge. The ยญยญ the Petitioner says ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What are the other two? You started off saying there were three. You got out the first. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Seeking interlocutory ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And I'm on ยญยญ on the edge of my seat, waiting to hear the other two. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: The second would be to accept dismissal, to say, Your Honor, I don't seek to file an amended plan, seek ยญยญ permit dismissal, which would have happened two days after ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Assume we don't think that's very effective. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Okay. That would be with seeking a stay. I think the party could seek an indicative ruling of whether a stay would be granted before choosing that option. And then the third opportunity would be to soยญcalled file the second amended plan, to file a plan that would get confirmed, and then take an appeal from that.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But that ยญยญ but you can see why that doesn't seem very good from anybody's point of view, including the system's point of view, right? You're going to file a plan that you don't like, that you don't want to live under, just in order to get an appeal right where you can come in and say, really what we want you to review is threeยญmonths ago when we had a different plan. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I think what's important is that it's not your preferred plan as the debtor. But it's not that you wouldn't be willing ยญยญ that it's not better than, for example, dismissal. Here in this case, the debtor chose to take an immediate appeal but, importantly, they moved the bankruptcy court to extend the time to file an amended plan until after the case came back from the court of appeals. This debtor wants to file another plan if the bankruptcy's decision is upheld, and that's because ยญยญ

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, can we go back to the route that you just described? Because it sounds strange to me. So the debtor's first plan is denied. He comes up with a second plan. Still in the second plan, the judge says, okay, he's in the position of being a winner. I don't know of a situation where somebody who ยญยญ who asks for certain relief, got it, can then appeal that relief, because there is a relief that would be more favorable. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Oh, oh, it happens all the time in civil litigation, Your Honor, because the interlocutory orders merge with the final order. So if the ยญยญ if ยญยญ in typical civil litigation, if the plaintiff believed that they were entitled to multiple damages or they believed that they were entitled to lost profits or some other measure of damages and instead they were awarded something, but lesser measure of damages ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, I take it some courts will do it and some courts won't. But even, let's say you're right, that all courts will say, okay, now, that we're here, I'll go and I'll look back at the plan that you really wanted. I mean, you are ยญยญ you said, well, it wasn't his first choice plan, but he's accepted it. But maybe he's accepted it just to get the appeal on the other plan. If he's stuck with that latter plan, he's actually going to bag the whole thing and go into Chapter 7 or something. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: I ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And you're just making people run through hoops and ยญยญ and agree to plans that nobody is willing to live under. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: I โ I ยญยญ actually think it's very unlikely. The facts of this case are, I think, a very good example. The debtor here, the entire premise of the proposed plan, is this debtor is entirely capable of making his monthly mortgage payment according to the terms of his mortgage. There are certain other unsecured creditors. And the debtor says, and I'm able to pay monthly $155 on those two. On a ยญยญ and then there are other creditors who failed to file proofs of claim. Those creditors which are in the tens of thousands of dollars, they're going to go away if a plan is confirmed, any plan is confirmed. So what this debtor could have done is say, okay, I'm going to keep making the same monthly payments just as I proposed, just as I should under my mortgage, but I'm not going to get to cram down to the bank. I'm not going to get to force them to take a haircut. I'm still going to get to pay off in a very regular way my other unsecured creditors $155 a month, that will be done in I think we calculated about 20 months. That is definitely a preferred plan than anything else, again, because those creditors who failed to file proofs of claim, they're gone. Tens of thousands of dollars of debt.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right. Now, I asked the other side this, I'd like to hear your answer, though it's more ยญยญ I ยญยญ this is a contested matter. Collier says there are endless contested matters. He lists about 47. All right. Now, when I asked, but does that mean in all those cases where there is an order in a contested matter, there will be an appeal? The answer was no. No. But this case is different because it has a beginning, middle, and an end, and it's a discrete proceeding. Now, is that, in your opinion, the correct test for sifting among contested matters? Are there other contested matters, where appeal, as if they were final orders, is allowed? What is the criterion? Will it, in fact, produce a nightmare? You're going to say, yes, but I mean, I'd like to know why. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, Your Honor, I guess I would start by answering that I don't think that contested matter is the ยญยญ the standard.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: They don't either. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, the United States did propose that ยญยญ that standard. And I think that we've shown that that can't possibly be correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: All right, then I think they might now agree. So what is the standard? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: So โ So ยญยญ we think that the proper question is, what is a proceeding?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ pursuant to the Bankruptcy Code. Section 157(b)(2) lists a number of proceedings, one of which is confirmations of plans, which I think is fully consistent with our view that it's confirmation that is the purpose of this, and until you get there, you haven't reached the end, especially when it's denied with leave to amend any small factual dispute, any ยญยญ it can be big or small. It can be many, many ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So far, it sounds to me as if you may agree with them that the correct test is, is it a discrete proceeding within the larger case, and there's nothing that will help me get more specific than that. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I think that their ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Am I right about ยญยญ on the standard of what counts as a final order for appeal? I can't ยญยญ I don't want to put words in your mouth ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: We ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: ยญยญ but that I can say there is basic agreement there. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I ยญยญ I would say that this ยญยญ there ยญยญ there has been in the briefing, at least, this amount of disagreement between us. We think that the word "finality" ยญยญ or "final," rather, as used in Section 158, the bankruptcy appeal provision, is the same as โfinalโ as it's used in ยญยญ in 1291 for civil appeals, that the judicial unit is different. It can be a proceeding as opposed to the whole case. But โfinalโ has been interpreted to mean terminates the judicial unit. The ยญยญ the Court has, in a sense, dissociated ยญยญ it's not, we're ยญยญ we're done with this one now. Okay? They have suggested that โfinalโ may mean something a little bit broader in bankruptcy or that the use of the word "order" instead of "decision" in bankruptcy may be something different. That argument there which ยญยญ and they've invoked this notion of flexible finality ยญยญ that should send off alarm bells in the Court's mind, because flexible finality is basically the same as a practical construction of finality, which is the clause that spawned the Collateral Order Doctrine which the Court has spent the last 30 years trying to put back in a box. And that is really the problem here; that unless there is a clear rule ยญยญ and we think it's the statutory language proceeding. We think Section 157 provides a list of such proceedings, and for these purposes, it's ยญยญ

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, but that ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ confirmation of a plan.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that is your ยญยญ it's still begging the question, because they're saying the ยญยญ the proceeding is the confirmation of a plan, and if the confirmation of the plan is a denial, that ยญยญ that ends that proceeding. So you are begging the question. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I ยญยญ I โ I don't think so, Your Honor, because I think that ยญยญ I'm drawing the analogy to 1291. I think final means the same thing. And in 1291, if you have a ยญยญ a complaint that's dismissed with leave to amend, that's not final. If you have a plan that's denied confirmation with leave to amend, that's not final. If you have a claim, a proof of claim, that is rejected with leave to amend or a claim of priority that isn't rejected with leave to amend, that's not final in our view. On their view, I don't know, because they assume that if you had a proof of claim, and it was denied with leave to amend, that must be immediately appealable as final, because now the creditors' preferred view of their claim has been rejected. Or maybe their rule is ยญยญ I don't know ยญยญ only if the basis of the denial was a rule of law. But their ยญยญ I think they rightly understand that you can't have a rule of finality that turns on whether it is or is not a rule of law, so as this Court's precedent instructs, we decide as a category. And as a category, plans are denied multiple times within a bankruptcy, many times for minute, factยญbased reasons. And, again, the incentive structure that would be created ยญยญ we've talked so far about Chapter 13 pretty exclusively, but the ยญยญ the Petitioner agreed that the rule would likely apply to Chapter 11, as well. In Chapter 11, things are quite different. In Chapter 11, the debtor is often the debtorยญinยญpossession is the estate. They're playing with house money. They're spending the creditors' money because the administrative expenses of the estate read the lawyers' fees come off of the estate. So for them to take an appeal on any issue that they can, you know, reasonably claim a legitimate dispute about and thereby prolong their control over the ยญยญ of the estate, prolong the date when distributions will be made ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And they have resources to do it. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: And they have the resources to do it.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: And Chapter 13 doesn't? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: That's right. But the rule is going to be the same. And in Chapter 13, what we've seen ยญยญ we didn't see the ยญยญ the ยญยญ the big numbers. We did see over seven hundred reported, or ยญยญ or at least on Westlaw, decisions in appeals that had been pursued pro se. So an individual debtor who is intent on preserving their ability to stay in the house certainly could take a number of appeals. Again, this case, I think, proves the point. This case started with an objection by the Trustee to the fact that this Debtor claimed an automobile ownership cost, the same one that was ultimately rejected by the Court in Ransom. That issue could have been taken up on immediate appeal. The second time they amended it was because the bank had objected that when they listed the payments that would be made on the mortgage, they only gave the principal and interest. They weren't going to pay the insurance or the taxes to escrow. That could have been taken all the way up. And then we had the question of the hybrid plan. That's setting aside any other disputes that might have existed about the value of the ยญยญ the home, whether Zillow is an appropriate measure. That was an issue of dispute between the parties. Any of those could have given rise to an appeal. And, again, there can be a defensive reason for taking an appeal because the ยญยญ the debtor wants to stay in the house to prevent foreclosure, or it could be offensive because they want those creditors to know they ยญยญ if they want to see their money, they better agree to the debtor's terms. So it's ยญยญ it's a significant concern, I think, and, again, because you're making these determinations on the basis of the category, it's important to remember that even those factual disputes could be ยญยญ and when we think of, you know ยญยญ which do we think is ยญยญ is ยญยญ is more unlikely? That the debtors are going to exercise restraint. Trust the debtors. They won't take appeals when they shouldn't be taken. Or do we trust the courts to apply the rules, the very liberal rules, for interlocutory review. And to do that ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. HallwardยญDriemeier, I mean ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ wisely ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: ยญยญ one of the things that confuses me about this case, quite honestly, is why you don't have more people on your side. In other words, where are the creditors, and where are the amicus briefs from the creditors who think that your position is important in order to prevent all of these appeals that you say are going to ruin the system? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, without, I hope, being too flippant, when the Bank of America is on the other side, a lot of creditors give pause as to whether they want to be adverse to them on our side. A lot of counsel have concern about whether they should file a brief on our side. But the ยญยญ I've already, I think, explained that the Bank of America, in their amicus brief, have explained their reason for wanting to get ยญยญ

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: ยญยญ reviewed.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: ยญยญ you explained a reason why a particular company, creditor, is on the other side. But, I mean, really, do you think everybody in the world is so intimidated by the Bank of America?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: That no ยญยญ

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Maybe they thought you could handle it on your own. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: I think ยญยญ you ยญยญ you know, Your Honor, it ยญยญ I think it ยญยญ it is actually interesting because here the Court has ยญยญ and again, I'm ยญยญ I'm borrowing from the Collateral Order Doctrine cases ยญยญ what the Court has stressed is that even though the parties may have an interest in being able to force the court of appeals to decide an issue, the judicial system has its own interest. The courts have their own interest. The court of appeals have an interest in not being forced to resolve issues before they need to be resolved because some of those issues are going to go away. If there is an appeal from a later plan that's ultimately confirmed, I would suspect strongly that there will be many disputes along the way that will have fallen out either because they became moot ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I thought that was the reason that ยญยญ that the ยญยญ the ยญยญ all are debating about what the incentives are. Who's going to take more appeals and who isn't really has nothing to do with it. What they ยญยญ world they actually face is where nobody takes appeals, period, from denials anyway. And ยญยญ and they say the bankruptcy judges are all over the lot on some of this, and they'd like to generate some appeals because they believe that then you would have a more uniform law. It would be easier for the creditors as well as the debtors. And I think that's their basic argument in their brief. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: It ยญยญ it ยญยญ it is their argument and ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I ยญยญ I don't know where to put that ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: ยญยญ in this ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: I guess I look at this Court's docket and it seems to me that bankruptcy cases are fairly well represented in the appellate courts. I ยญยญ I do agree that there are issues on which parties would like to get reviewed, but Congress has considered that very issue. And in the statute ยญยญ in 2005, at a time when it was effectively 5ยญ0 our way on this issue. The only question was the Fifth Circuit. But, again, they had suggested that if leave was given to amend, it would not be final. When Congress looked at it in that context, they didn't change the final order rule which had been construed by five courts of appeals at that time. What they did was they created a new avenue for interlocutory appeal direct to the court of appeals precisely to get that kind of guidance, but ยญยญ but they gave the power to the court of appeals to decide whether the issue was coming up ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: So one ยญยญ one thing. If you were ยญยญ maybe there's an argument against this ยญยญ but suppose for argument's sake I agreed with you on the basic point. It would then seem important to put in the opinion there is a problem here about there being insufficient appeals to generate law, but there is a mechanism, namely, the interlocutory mechanism, which perhaps has been used too sparingly. Now, what do you think of that? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: I ยญยญ I think that would be very wise on the Court's part.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That was Justice Kagan's point initially. MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Because ยญยญ and ยญยญ and I think we should ยญยญ should recall that under Section 158, the first level of review which is exercised by the district court over interlocutory orders is really plenary. They can review any interlocutory order of the bankruptcy court.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You know, sometimes they even ignore our holdings. Do you think they're not going to ignore this ยญยญ

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: ยญยญ this ยญยญ this piece of advice? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, Your ยญยญ Your Honor ยญยญ MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: I think that the ยญยญ I think that this ยญยญ this approach is the same one the Court took in Mohawk. In Mohawk, the Court said, No, we're not going to create an appeal as of right to challenge in order to disclose one's attorneyยญclient confidential material, but ยญยญ but the courts should apply the rules of interlocutory appeal or mandamus in a way ยญยญ

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And did they take our advice? MR. HALLWARDยญDRIEMEIER: Well, I have not ยญยญ I have not researched that, Your Honor. But to conclude, I think that the critical question is what's the proceeding. And the proceeding as set forth in 157(b)(2) is confirmation of plans. That is the core purpose of a Chapter 13 or a Chapter 11. Until that happens, there are no legal rights that are affected. That's the difference between denial of plan confirmation with leave to amend and confirmation. One sets rights. Of course, it's immediately appealable. Res judicata, even, under this Court's decision in Espinosa, but denial of plan confirmation with leave the amend maybe even, as Your Honor suggested earlier ยญยญ Justice Breyer, suggesting precisely how to amend in order to get it confirmed, that is not immediately appealable. And I don't think that the Court should rely on the selfยญrestraint of the parties to avoid the problems with that problem. Instead, it's proper use of the tools that Congress gave the courts in the 2005 amendments. If there are no further questions, thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Feldman, two minutes.

turns.name: FELDMAN
turns.text: Thank you. For ยญยญ just one thing on ยญยญ on Section 158(a), the plenary, the ยญยญ so what my friend said was plenary right of interlocutory review, actually every court that has looked at that has said ยญยญ they applied the same standard as under 1292(b). But the main point here is that what the debtor is faced with is two very, very difficult roads to actually getting review of what could be an important legal decision. On the dismissal side, in addition to the problems caused by losing the automatic stay and trying to convince the court to grant a stay, there's also the disability of if you file ยญยญ file within ยญยญ what is it? A year, I think. The automatic ยญยญ if you refile a case, you only get an automatic stay for 30 days. And there's other disabilities that we talk about in our brief. So that is a very hard thing to impose on somebody where the creditor can make ยญยญ take an appeal just as of right because they don't like what the confirmed plan said. On the other hand, as far as trying to confirm a later plan, what this forces the debtor into is a situation where a debtor may have to try to figure out and propose a plan that the debtor might even prefer not to be in bankruptcy at all rather than to live under that plan. But yet, the only way for the debtor to get the review of the earlier denial of confirmation is to propose some plan that maybe disposes of property in a way that the debtor completely doesn't want and would rather have just given up on the whole thing. Nonetheless, they have to propose the plan, wait for objections, litigate it through, get it confirmed, and then appeal that. And that ยญยญ those are severe burdens to put on the debtor. Now, every ยญยญ the reasons why Bank of America and the United States are here, I think, is that there is a need for bankruptcy precedent to be developed. And one problem is that debtors in this situation are not able to get the review of the court of appeals, which is their only way of getting actually to an Article III judge at all. And they're not able to get that, and for those reasons, we ask that the decision of the court of appeals should be reversed.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
